video_title: Peter Boockvar: The AI Spending Bubble Is Cracking
gist: Peter Boockvar tells Wealthion's Maggie Lake that the AI trade's bubble sits in hyperscaler capex spending rather than the underlying technology, that Chinese DRAM (CXMT) and LLM makers are an underappreciated competitive threat to US chip and software leaders, and that rising real rates driven by 'debts and deficits' -- not inflation or labor strength -- are pressuring bonds and the dollar even as he still calls gold a buy on its pullback.

themes.theme: AI Capex Bubble and Earnings Quality

insights.claim: Peter Boockvar argues the AI trade's bubble is concentrated in hyperscaler capital spending, not in the underlying AI technology itself, which he expects users of the technology to ultimately benefit from.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Investors should separate capex-exposed spenders from technology beneficiaries when sizing risk in the AI trade rather than treating it as one uniform bet.
insights.quote: the bubble is clearly in the capex side, not necessarily the technology

insights.claim: Oracle's stock cracked after investors realized roughly half of its remaining performance obligations (RPO) backlog was tied to OpenAI, while Oracle's capex-to-revenue ratio climbed from about 50% to 75% and is now near 100% of revenue.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: A capex ratio approaching 100% of revenue signals thin financial cushion and single-customer concentration risk, a red flag for other hyperscalers with similar buildouts.
insights.quote: you're spending half your revenue on capex, which then went to 75% of your revenue on capex and now is 100% of your revenue on capex.

insights.claim: Meta's stock was hammered after reporting what Boockvar called 'stellar' earnings last year, purely on investor worries about the scale of its AI capex spend, an early sign the market had begun punishing capex-heavy names.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Strong headline earnings no longer protect capex-heavy AI names from selloffs once capex intensity becomes the market's focal risk.

insights.claim: Within the AI trade, markets have rotated from punishing the roughly $700+ billion of hyperscaler capex spenders to rewarding recipients of that spend -- first Nvidia and Broadcom, then memory and storage names, then server and data-center equipment sellers like Dell and HP.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Investors can track which link of the AI supply chain the market currently favors by watching this rotation pattern rather than assuming uniform AI-theme performance.
insights.quote: markets certainly started to differentiate between the 700 plus billion of spend and let's punish those that are spending it and let's reward the recipients of that spend

insights.claim: Q1 aggregate earnings growth of 28% is misleading: about 12 percentage points came from 'other income' -- mark-to-market gains hyperscalers booked on their equity stakes in SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI -- and roughly half of the remaining growth came from semiconductor earnings, leaving true underlying earnings growth in the mid-to-high single digits.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Headline S&P earnings growth is inflated by two narrow, non-repeatable drivers; investors should strip these out to gauge the real earnings trend heading into Q2 reports.
insights.quote: 12 percentage points of the 28% was other income with these big hyperscalers. other income being the mark-to-market gains that these companies have in their ownership of SpaceX, Anthropic, and Open AI.

insights.claim: Boockvar is skeptical that Micron's roughly 85% gross margin is sustainable, given memory has historically been a highly cyclical, commoditized business.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Margin peaks in cyclical semis historically precede compression; an 85% gross margin should be read as a warning sign, not a strength.

insights.claim: Boockvar's framework for valuing cyclical stocks like Micron runs opposite to how cheap multiples are usually read: a high PE signals the company is under-earning in a down-cycle (a buy signal), while a low PE signals peak, unsustainable margins (a caution signal).
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Investors should value Micron and similar cyclicals on price-to-book or price-to-sales rather than trailing PE, especially when margins look historically elevated.
insights.quote: never tell me what a PE ratio is on a cyclical stock and tell me it's cheap.

insights.claim: Micron traded at roughly 10 times sales at its recent peak, matching the same 9-10x sales peak multiple memory/semiconductor stocks hit in 1999-2000 just before that cycle turned down.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: A price-to-sales multiple matching the dot-com-era peak is a historical warning marker for cyclical semiconductor stocks.

insights.claim: Only about 40% of Micron's memory revenue is locked into long-term volume contracts, and even those contracts set a price ceiling and floor rather than fixed pricing, leaving roughly 60% of revenue exposed to open-market price swings.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Bulls citing locked-in contracts overstate Micron's earnings visibility since the majority of revenue still floats with spot pricing.
insights.quote: 40% is locked up, which means 60% of the revenue is still subject to the vagaries of pricing

insights.claim: Boockvar predicts the current DRAM cycle will reverse within the next couple of years: memory demand volume will keep rising, as it has since the mid-1990s, but pricing will fall as new supply comes online, compressing Micron's earnings even with its current supply contracts.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Positions in Micron or memory names should have an exit plan well before the 2027-2028 supply wave that bulls cite as the safe window.
insights.quote: you can be sure in the next couple years. This DEM cycle will reverse the volume demand will continue up but the pricing will go down and you will see a crushing of Micron's earnings.

insights.claim: Semiconductors now make up about 18-19% of the S&P 500, meaning index investors carry outsized, largely unavoidable exposure to the sector's cyclicality.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Passive index investors are structurally more exposed to a semiconductor downturn than they may realize.
insights.quote: semis in the US now make up about 18 to n 19% of the S&P 500.

themes.theme: China's AI Software and Hardware Threat

insights.claim: Chinese LLM developers -- DeepSeek, Alibaba's Qwen, MiniMax, and Zhipu -- are taking software market share by offering models built via distillation at roughly 75-80% lower cost than comparable US models.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Pricing pressure from cheap, good-enough Chinese models threatens the revenue assumptions underpinning US AI infrastructure capex.
insights.quote: A fraction being defined as 75 to 80% cheaper than US models.

insights.claim: CXMT's Hong Kong IPO, as the world's fourth-largest DRAM producer, received almost no US investor or media attention even as SK Hynix's listing dominated coverage the same week -- a gap Boockvar flags as a blind spot for US memory investors.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: US memory investors underpricing Chinese DRAM supply risk could be blindsided on future margin and market-share erosion in names like Micron.
insights.quote: No one's talking about the IPO, I think on Thursday of the week that we're taping this of CXMT uh, in Hong Kong, which is the fourth largest global DRAM producer.

insights.claim: Yangtze Memory Technology is emerging as a China-based competitive threat to NAND storage incumbents SanDisk and Western Digital, following the same market-share-first strategy as CXMT in DRAM.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Investors in NAND storage names should track Chinese entrant share gains the same way they should for DRAM.

insights.claim: Boockvar frames Chinese memory competitors as prioritizing market share over profit margin, echoing Jeff Bezos's 'your profit margin is my opportunity' -- meaning Micron's high current gross margins effectively invite Chinese entrants to undercut on price.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: High incumbent margins in a commodity business are themselves the signal that share-losing price competition is coming.
insights.quote: your profit margin is my opportunity

insights.claim: Because Chinese AI software models are open source, Boockvar sees fewer national-security objections to their adoption than on the hardware side, meaning the real competitive battle is for the 'global customer' -- price-sensitive companies in Europe, Latin America, and Asia -- rather than a purely bilateral US-China contest.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: US AI software leaders face share loss abroad from cheaper Chinese alternatives even without a formal security ban, especially among budget-constrained smaller companies.
insights.quote: It's the competition for the global customer.

insights.claim: Boockvar argues US tariff policy inadvertently forced foreign companies and countries to diversify their supply chains away from US suppliers to protect margins and input costs, a structural shift that persists regardless of one's view on the tariffs themselves.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: US exporters and suppliers may see permanent overseas share loss even if tariffs are later reduced, since alternate supply chains tend to stick once built.
insights.quote: what we did to foreign countries and companies was force them to diversify their supply chains.

themes.theme: Sector Rotation: Banks and Consumer Staples

insights.claim: Bank stocks are rallying globally for different regional reasons: Japanese banks benefit from a steep yield curve as the BOJ slow-walks short-rate hikes, European banks are helped by the ECB raising rates away from negative territory, and US banks are getting a boost from strong capital markets activity and a widening yield spread since Kevin Warsh's first Fed meeting.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: The bank rally is a genuine multi-region trend, not a single-country phenomenon, supporting financials as a diversification sleeve away from AI-heavy positioning.

insights.claim: Boockvar holds Kraft Heinz as a contrarian consumer-staples value pick: the stock has been a disaster since the 3G Capital merger and only 1 of 22 covering analysts rates it a buy, but he cites a new CEO reinvesting in the business and a well-covered, generous dividend yield as reasons it is now dirt cheap and due for stabilization.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Deeply unloved staples with sell-side consensus almost universally bearish can offer asymmetric value if fundamentals are stabilizing.
insights.quote: you have 22 analysts that cover it... and only one has it as a buy.

insights.claim: Pepsi's stock fell on earnings despite showing that while its lower-income-skewed US consumer business remains challenged, its international business is performing well and overindexing growth relative to the US -- a pattern Boockvar expects across global food companies.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Investors punishing global staples purely on weak US consumer data may be missing offsetting international growth already showing up in results.

insights.claim: Investors are rotating out of hyperscalers on deteriorating cash-flow worries and out of overindexed semiconductor exposure into consumer staples and healthcare stocks as diversification plays.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Broadening participation beyond AI-linked names into staples and healthcare is an early signal of a market-wide de-risking rotation.

insights.claim: Boockvar expects Fed Chair Kevin Warsh to hold rates steady for the rest of the year, caught between political pressure from the Trump administration against hiking and the need to avoid appearing too dovish, while the inflation- and data-measurement task forces he created won't report until year-end, giving him cover to do nothing.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Rate-sensitive positioning should assume Fed inaction through year-end rather than pricing in either cuts or hikes.

insights.claim: US 10-year Treasury real rates are rising even though TIPS-implied inflation expectations remain muted and the labor market is only mediocre, a combination Boockvar says he cannot fully explain by either inflation fears or labor-market strength.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: A real-rate rise untethered from the usual growth/inflation drivers points to a supply-and-issuance story investors should watch rather than dismiss.
insights.quote: this rise in real rates is rather fascinating... I can't necessarily connect the dot to new found worries about inflation.

insights.claim: Boockvar believes 'debts and deficits now matter' for the first time in his career, with bond markets choking on excess supply driven by hyperscaler debt issuance and rising government spending -- US defense, German defense, and Japanese and UK fiscal expansion -- pushing up long-term yields globally.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: A structural, supply-driven rise in long yields argues for favoring shorter duration and inflation-protected assets over long government bonds.
insights.quote: I think maybe markets are choking on excess supply for the first time in my lifetime.

insights.claim: Boockvar calls the dollar a sell: even though the market has swung from pricing two Fed rate cuts to about one-and-a-half rate hikes this year, the dollar has only rallied modestly from 97 to 101, and he expects that modest rally to roll over once the real-rate rise ends and inflation catches up to nominal rates.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: The dollar's muted response to a large hawkish repricing suggests limited further upside and supports fading dollar strength.
insights.quote: If this is if we entered the year with the Fed funds futures market pricing in two rate cuts now we are pricing in about one and a half rate increases and the dollar went from 97 to 101. That's not really much of a rally.

themes.theme: Gold and Japan Capital Repatriation Risk

insights.claim: Boockvar views gold's recent pullback as driven specifically by rising real rates, not a change in the metal's underlying thesis, and calls it a buy on this pullback after its earlier straight-line run made a rest period inevitable.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Frames the gold dip as a real-rate-driven pause rather than a trend reversal, i.e. a buying opportunity if real rates stabilize or fall.
insights.quote: I think gold's a buy on this pullback.

insights.claim: The yen has fallen to about 162 per dollar, a 40-year low, as the Bank of Japan continues to slow-crawl short-term rate hikes relative to inflation, a dynamic that is itself further weakening the yen and fueling inflation.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Continued BOJ policy lag versus inflation sets up further yen weakness until the central bank accelerates hikes.

insights.claim: Japan's GPIF, the world's largest pension fund with $1.8 trillion in assets, and Japan more broadly (the third-largest net creditor nation with a $3.5 trillion net investment position) face growing pressure to repatriate overseas capital as JGB yields become more attractive than US Treasuries or French OATs -- a shift Boockvar warns could create major flow-of-funds effects for global bond and equity markets given how much Japan owns abroad, even for investors with no direct Japan exposure.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Even a marginal reallocation by GPIF (whose roughly 25/25/25/25 split across domestic/foreign equities and bonds leaves room to shift) could pressure US Treasuries and global equities, making Japanese repatriation signals worth monitoring regardless of direct Japan exposure.
insights.quote: Bring some home. We have a great stock market. We have a cheap currency and we have these now attractive JGB yields.
